title: Ephesians 6
subtitle: Household Duties and the Armour of God

heb.paragraph: The verb timaō in v. 2 is drawn from the fifth commandment (Exod 20:12): ‘Honour your father and mother.’ Paul identifies this as ‘the first commandment with a promise’ (v. 2)—the promise of well-being and long life. The honour extends beyond obedience to include respect, provision, and care.

The word kyrios functions with double reference in the slave-master instructions (vv. 5–9): slaves serve their earthly masters ‘as you would obey Christ’ (v. 5), knowing that ‘the Lord will reward each one’ (v. 8). Masters are reminded that they have the same Master in heaven (v. 9). The christological reframing of the master-slave relationship relativises earthly authority under divine sovereignty.

refs.note: The parallel household code in Colossians addresses the same relationships (children/parents, slaves/masters) with nearly identical language, though Ephesians develops each instruction more fully.
The fifth commandment quoted in Eph 6:2–3 provides the OT foundation for the parent-child relationship. Paul identifies it as ‘the first commandment with a promise.’
Paul’s letter to Philemon addresses the slave-master relationship concretely, asking Philemon to receive Onesimus ‘no longer as a slave, but better than a slave, as a dear brother.’

notes.note: Children’s obedience and honour toward parents are grounded in the Decalogue. MacArthur notes the balance: children obey, but fathers do not provoke. Parenting is positive (‘bring them up in the training and instruction of the Lord’), not merely restrictive.

Paul does not endorse slavery as an institution but transforms it from within. Slaves serve their earthly masters ‘as you would obey Christ’—elevating their work to worship. Masters are told to ‘treat your slaves in the same way’—with the same respect and accountability. The reminder that ‘there is no favouritism with him’ (v. 9) is subversive: before God, the master-slave distinction is meaningless.

Calvin emphasises that the commandment to honour parents carries a promise: longevity and prosperity. Calvin reads this not as a guarantee of individual outcomes but as a general principle of divine blessing on societies that honour the family order. The father’s duty is to nurture, not to tyrannise: provocation destroys what instruction is meant to build.

Calvin reads the slave-master instructions as an example of how the gospel operates within existing social structures: not by immediate revolution but by internal transformation. The slave serves Christ through his master; the master answers to Christ for his treatment of his slave. The hierarchy is not abolished but relativised under the lordship of Christ.

The phrase hētis estin entolē prōtē en epaggelia (which is the first commandment with a promise) has been debated: is it the first in sequence, the first in importance, or the first with an attached promise? The NET translators favour the last option: it is the first commandment in the Decalogue to carry an explicit promise of blessing.

The phrase prosōpolēmpsia (favouritism, partiality) appears also in Rom 2:11 and Col 3:25. The NET notes that it is a NT coinage formed from the LXX phrase lambanein prosōpon (to lift the face, i.e., show favouritism).

Lincoln reads the household code as shaped by the reciprocal submission principle of 5:21. Each pair of instructions balances obligation with accountability. Lincoln notes that the slave-master instructions are the longest section of the code, reflecting the pastoral urgency of addressing this relationship in a community where slaves and masters worshipped together.

O’Brien emphasises the christological transformation of each relationship. In the parent-child instructions, obedience is ‘in the Lord’ and training is ‘of the Lord.’ In the slave-master instructions, service is rendered ‘as to the Lord’ and masters answer to the Lord. O’Brien argues that this christological reframing does not merely Christianise existing social structures but introduces a fundamentally new dynamic that will eventually subvert the institution of slavery from within.

hist.context: The household code continues from 5:22–33 with instructions for children and parents (vv. 1–4) and slaves and masters (vv. 5–9). Children are to obey their parents ‘in the Lord’ (v. 1)—a christological qualifier that transforms familial obedience into an act of worship. Fathers are warned against exasperating their children and instructed instead to bring them up ‘in the training and instruction of the Lord’ (v. 4). The slave-master instructions (vv. 5–9) are remarkable for their christological transformation of the institution: slaves serve ‘as slaves of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart’ (v. 6), and masters are reminded that they share the same Lord (v. 9). The reciprocal obligations subvert the social hierarchy from within while leaving the institution formally intact.

heb.gloss: full armour, complete set of weapons
heb.paragraph: The noun panoplia (vv. 11, 13) denotes the complete equipment of a heavy-armed soldier (hoplite). Paul’s use of the term emphasises that no piece may be omitted: the spiritual battle requires comprehensive preparation. Each piece of armour corresponds to a spiritual reality: truth, righteousness, the gospel of peace, faith, salvation, and the word of God.

The noun palē (v. 12) denotes close hand-to-hand combat, not distant warfare. Paul’s point: the spiritual battle is not impersonal or detached but intimate and intense. The opponents are not ‘flesh and blood’ but cosmic spiritual powers: rulers, authorities, powers of this dark world, and spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms.

refs.note: Isaiah’s description of God putting on righteousness as a breastplate and the helmet of salvation on his head provides the OT source for Paul’s armour imagery. Paul transfers the warrior-God’s equipment to believers who share in God’s battle against evil.
Paul’s earlier use of armour imagery—‘putting on faith and love as a breastplate, and the hope of salvation as a helmet’—provides a compressed parallel to the fuller description in Eph 6:10–17.

Paul’s statement that ‘the weapons we fight with are not the weapons of the world’ but have divine power to demolish strongholds provides the conceptual framework for the spiritual warfare of Eph 6:10–20.

notes.note: The call to ‘be strong in the Lord’ acknowledges that the believer’s strength is derivative, not inherent. The enemy is not human but supernatural: cosmic powers of evil operating in the heavenly realms. MacArthur notes that the spiritual battle is real, ongoing, and cannot be won by human resources.

Each piece of armour represents an aspect of the believer’s spiritual resources. Truth is the foundation (belt); righteousness is the moral integrity that protects the heart (breastplate); the gospel of peace provides firm footing (shoes); faith extinguishes the enemy’s attacks (shield); salvation guards the mind (helmet); the word of God is the only offensive weapon (sword). MacArthur notes that five pieces are defensive and one is offensive, reflecting the believer’s primary posture of standing firm rather than attacking.

Prayer is not a seventh piece of armour but the spiritual activity that undergirds all the others. MacArthur notes the comprehensiveness: ‘all kinds of prayers and requests’ (v. 18), ‘on all occasions’ (v. 18), ‘for all the Lord’s people’ (v. 18). Paul’s specific request for boldness in preaching (vv. 19–20) reveals his own dependence on the church’s intercession.

Calvin reads the spiritual warfare passage as a corrective to both presumption and despair. The believer’s strength is in the Lord—not in self-effort. The enemy’s power is real but limited: he operates only within the bounds God permits. Calvin notes that the battle is against ‘spiritual forces of evil,’ not against flesh and blood: the church’s weapons are spiritual, not political or military.

Calvin assigns specific theological content to each piece of armour. Truth is the integrity of the whole person before God; righteousness is the imputed righteousness of Christ that protects the conscience from Satan’s accusations; the gospel of peace provides stability in trial; faith is the instrument by which divine promises are grasped; salvation is the hope of final deliverance; the sword of the Spirit is the word of God by which temptation is resisted (cf. Jesus in Matt 4:1–11).

The four terms for cosmic powers—archas (rulers), exousias (authorities), kosmokratoras (world rulers), pneumatika tēs ponērias (spiritual forces of evil)—represent an ascending hierarchy of malevolent spiritual beings. The NET translators note that kosmokratōr is used in astrological texts for planetary spirits controlling earthly affairs.

The phrase tēn machairan tou pneumatos (the sword of the Spirit) uses a genitive of source: the Spirit provides the sword. The appositional clause ho estin rhēma theou identifies this sword as the spoken word of God—rhēma (utterance) rather than logos (word), suggesting specific, situation-appropriate application of Scripture.

Lincoln reads the armour passage as the culmination of the letter’s cosmic theology. The ‘heavenly realms’ that are the location of spiritual blessings (1:3) and Christ’s exaltation (1:20) are also the arena of spiritual conflict (6:12). Lincoln notes the defensive emphasis: the repeated command to ‘stand’ (stēnai, vv. 11, 13, 14) implies maintaining the position Christ has won, not conquering new territory. The prayer section (vv. 18–20) is integral: prayer is the atmosphere in which the armour is effective.

O’Brien identifies the passage as the practical climax of the entire letter. The cosmic powers introduced in 1:21 and 3:10 are now identified as the church’s adversaries. O’Brien notes that the armour imagery is drawn primarily from Isaiah (11:5; 49:2; 52:7; 59:17), where God himself is the warrior. Paul transfers the divine warrior’s equipment to believers, who share in God’s battle by virtue of their union with the exalted Christ. The prayer section (vv. 18–20) is not an appendix but the means by which the armour is activated and the community is strengthened for battle.

hist.context: The armour of God passage (vv. 10–20) is the climactic section of Ephesians, drawing together the letter’s themes of cosmic powers (1:21; 3:10), the heavenly realms (1:3, 20; 2:6; 3:10), and the believers’ union with the exalted Christ. The call to ‘be strong in the Lord’ (v. 10) introduces the military metaphor, which is developed through six pieces of armour drawn from Isaiah’s description of God as a warrior (Isa 11:5; 59:17) and the Roman legionary’s equipment. The belt of truth (v. 14a), the breastplate of righteousness (v. 14b), the shoes of the gospel of peace (v. 15), the shield of faith (v. 16), the helmet of salvation (v. 17a), and the sword of the Spirit (v. 17b) form a comprehensive spiritual arsenal. The passage concludes with a call to prayer (vv. 18–20)—the spiritual discipline that activates the armour.

heb.paragraph: The final word of the letter’s benediction is en aphtharsia (v. 24)—‘with an undying love’ or ‘in immortality.’ The term adds an eschatological note: the grace and love that characterise the believers’ relationship with Christ are imperishable, extending beyond death into eternity.

refs.note: The nearly identical commendation of Tychicus confirms that both letters were sent at the same time and carried by the same messenger.
Paul’s closing benediction—‘the Lord be with your spirit. Grace be with you all’—provides a parallel to the grace-benediction pattern of Eph 6:24.

notes.note: Tychicus is described as ‘the dear brother and faithful servant in the Lord’—the same description as in Col 4:7. MacArthur notes the importance of trustworthy letter-carriers in the early church: they provided context, answered questions, and reported on the apostle’s condition.

The benediction invokes peace and love with faith—the trinity of theological virtues that characterise the community Paul has described throughout the letter. The final phrase ‘with an undying love’ adds the eschatological dimension: the love that binds believers to Christ is not temporary but eternal.

Calvin reads the benediction as a summary of the letter’s theology: peace (the reconciliation achieved in Christ), love (the imitatio Dei of ch. 5), faith (the means of appropriating all spiritual blessings), and grace (the source of everything from election to glorification). The phrase ‘in immortality’ or ‘with an undying love’ guarantees that the realities described in the letter are not provisional but permanent.

The final prepositional phrase en aphtharsia is debated: it may modify ‘love’ (those who love the Lord with an imperishable love) or ‘grace’ (grace be with all in immortality). The NET translators favour the connection with love, producing ‘all who love our Lord Jesus Christ with an undying love.’

Lincoln notes the conventionality of the closing but identifies the unique element: the modifier en aphtharsia transforms a standard grace-wish into an eschatological affirmation. The letter’s vision of cosmic reconciliation in Christ is not a temporary arrangement but an imperishable reality.

O’Brien reads the closing as a pastoral gesture: after the cosmic theology and demanding ethics of the letter, Paul grounds the community in the concrete reality of a fellow-believer (Tychicus) and the assurance of divine grace. The letter’s final word—aphtharsia—points beyond the present evil age to the consummation of God’s eternal purpose.

hist.context: The letter’s closing is brief. Paul commends Tychicus as the bearer of the letter and source of personal news (vv. 21–22)—the same role Tychicus plays for Colossians (Col 4:7–8). The benediction (vv. 23–24) invokes peace, love with faith, and grace for all who love the Lord Jesus Christ ‘with an undying love.’ The letter that began with ‘every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms’ (1:3) closes with grace that is imperishable, forming an inclusio of divine generosity.

themes.note: Faith and Sovereignty share the highest scores as the armour of God passage emphasises trusting in divine power against cosmic evil. Holiness is prominent through the household code’s ethical demands. Mission is elevated through the prayer for boldness in proclamation.

rows.label: vv.1–4
rows.text: Children and parents: obey, honour; do not provoke; train

rows.label: vv.5–9
rows.text: Slaves and masters: serve as Christ; no favouritism

rows.label: vv.10–12
rows.text: Be strong in the Lord: the battle is spiritual, not physical

rows.label: vv.13–17
rows.text: The full armour: truth, righteousness, gospel, faith, salvation, word

rows.label: vv.18–20
rows.text: Prayer: the atmosphere of spiritual warfare

rows.label: vv.23–24
rows.text: Benediction: peace, love, faith, grace in immortality

lit.note: Ephesians 6 completes the household code (vv. 1–9) and climaxes with the armour of God passage (vv. 10–20). The letter’s cosmic theology finds its practical application in spiritual warfare: the powers introduced in 1:21 are now the church’s adversaries, and the divine resources celebrated in chs. 1–3 are now the church’s armour.

hebtext.note: The verb timaō in v. 2 is drawn from the fifth commandment (Exod 20:12): ‘Honour your father and mother.’ Paul identifies this as ‘the first commandment with a promise’ (v. 2)—the promise of well-being and long life. The honour extends beyond obedience to include respect, provision, and care.

The word kyrios functions with double reference in the slave-master instructions (vv. 5–9): slaves serve their earthly masters ‘as you would obey Christ’ (v. 5), knowing that ‘the Lord will reward each one’ (v. 8). Masters are reminded that they have the same Master in heaven (v. 9). The christological reframing of the master-slave relationship relativises earthly authority under divine sovereignty.

hebtext.gloss: full armour, complete set of weapons
hebtext.note: The noun panoplia (vv. 11, 13) denotes the complete equipment of a heavy-armed soldier (hoplite). Paul’s use of the term emphasises that no piece may be omitted: the spiritual battle requires comprehensive preparation. Each piece of armour corresponds to a spiritual reality: truth, righteousness, the gospel of peace, faith, salvation, and the word of God.

The noun palē (v. 12) denotes close hand-to-hand combat, not distant warfare. Paul’s point: the spiritual battle is not impersonal or detached but intimate and intense. The opponents are not ‘flesh and blood’ but cosmic spiritual powers: rulers, authorities, powers of this dark world, and spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms.

The final word of the letter’s benediction is en aphtharsia (v. 24)—‘with an undying love’ or ‘in immortality.’ The term adds an eschatological note: the grace and love that characterise the believers’ relationship with Christ are imperishable, extending beyond death into eternity.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

thread.anchor: Household Duties and the Armour of God
thread.text: Paul’s earlier use of armour imagery—‘putting on faith and love as a breastplate, and the hope of salvation as a helmet’—provides a compressed parallel to the fuller description in Eph 6:10–17.

thread.anchor: Household Duties and the Armour of God
thread.text: Paul’s statement that ‘the weapons we fight with are not the weapons of the world’ but have divine power to demolish strongholds provides the conceptual framework for the spiritual warfare of Eph 6:10–20.

discourse.thesis: Live out the household relationships in the Lord; take up the full armor of God to stand against spiritual opposition — and keep praying for one another and for boldness to make the gospel known.

nodes.text: The household code continues: children obey parents in the Lord (first commandment with a promise, Exod 20:12); fathers do not provoke children to anger but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord; slaves obey earthly masters as to Christ, not by way of eye-service but as servants of Christ doing the will of God from the heart; masters treat slaves the same way, knowing that the Master of both is in heaven.

children.text: The motivation for work: rendering service with good will as to the Lord and not to men, knowing that whatever good anyone does, this he will receive back from the Lord. The eschatological reward transforms the character of even the most menial labor — every worker is accountable to the same Lord.

nodes.text: The armor of God: be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might. Put on (ἐνδύσασθε) the full armor (πανοπλία) of God — not Roman armor reimagined, but the armor God himself wears in Isaiah 59:17. The opponents are not flesh and blood but rulers (ἀρχάς), authorities (ἐξουσίας), cosmic powers (κοσμοκράτορας) of this present darkness, spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places.

children.text: The six pieces of armor: belt of truth, breastplate of righteousness, shoes of the gospel of peace, shield of faith (to extinguish the devil's flaming darts), helmet of salvation, sword of the Spirit (which is the word of God). Five are defensive; the sword is the one offensive weapon — the spoken word of God.

The battle is spiritual, not political or social: 'not against flesh and blood.' This does not dematerialize the conflict — the spiritual powers express themselves through political, social, and material structures. The armor equips the church to stand firm (στῆναι, three times in vv. 11-14) rather than attack.

nodes.text: Praying at all times in the Spirit (ἐν πνεύματι) — with all prayer and supplication, keeping alert with all perseverance for all the saints. And specifically: for Paul, that words may be given to him to open his mouth boldly (ἐν παρρησίᾳ) to proclaim the mystery of the gospel for which he is an ambassador in chains.

children.text: Paul's self-description as 'ambassador in chains' (πρεσβεύω ἐν ἁλύσει) is deliberately paradoxical — an ambassador represents the sovereign power of his sender, yet Paul is imprisoned. His boldness would demonstrate that the gospel's power is not limited by his circumstance.

nodes.text: Tychicus the beloved brother will inform them of Paul's situation and encourage their hearts. Final benediction: peace, love, and faith from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ, and grace to all who love our Lord Jesus Christ with love incorruptible (ἐν ἀφθαρσίᾳ).

children.text: The closing benediction mirrors the opening: the letter began with grace and peace (1:2) and ends with grace, love, and incorruptibility. The circular structure envelops the entire letter in the same divine gift — grace that is inexhaustible.

discourse.note: Ephesians 6 completes the household code (vv. 1-9) and then dramatically reframes the entire ethical section by revealing its ultimate context: the battle is cosmic, not merely domestic. The armor passage (vv. 10-17) is the eschatological frame for all the preceding ethics — the virtues Paul has urged (humility, love, truth-speaking, forgiveness) are the very armor of God. The letter ends with prayer as the capstone of the armor, and with Tychicus as the living letter alongside the written one.

coaching.tip: Household codes (vv. 1-9) regulate existing structures—children/parents, slaves/masters—without abolishing them. Yet Paul subverts from within: masters answer to a heavenly Master; the 'Lord' slaves serve isn't Caesar. Revolutionary seeds planted in conventional soil.

'Our struggle is not against flesh and blood' (v. 12). The armor of God (vv. 13-17) equips for spiritual combat. Notice: every piece is defensive except one—'the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God.' The church advances by proclamation, not aggression.
